comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is almost here and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my seventy-first published book and my first venture into what used to be a very common genre, the commentary on a spiritual classic. Dion Fortune's The Cosmic Doctrine is to my mind the most important volume of occult philosophy from the 20th century; it's as important as it is difficult to read, and I decided to put up monthly commentaries on each chapter on my blog. That turned into a very solid book, which many readers have already told me makes a good introduction to Fortune's work. A Commentary on the Cosmic Doctrine can be found here if you live in the US and here if you live elsewhere. 

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
Happy Astrological New Year!

At this link is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts. Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below and/or in the comments at the current prayer list post.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.


May Liz and her baby be blessed and healthy during pregnancy, and may her husband Jay (sdi) have the grace and good humor to support his family despite stress and ill health.

May Trubujah/David's nephew Jace, who is in hospital with a fever at a time when is immune system is compromised due to chemotherapy, heal quickly from this fever, and may he and his mother and family be lent strength spiritually, mentally, and physically through this process.

May 1 Wanderer's partner Cathy, who has bravely fought against cancer to the stage of remission, now be relieved of the unpleasant and painful side-effects from the follow-up hormonal treatment, together with the stress that this imposes on both parties, and may she quickly be able to resume a normal life.

May Jennifer’s newborn daughter Eleanor be blessed with optimal growth and development; may her tongue tie revision surgery on Wednesday March 12th have been smooth and successful, and be followed by a full recovery.

May Goats and Roses' son A, who had a serious concussion weeks ago and is still suffering from the effects, regain normal healthy brain function, and rebuild his physical strength back to normal, and regain his zest for life. And may Goats and Roses be granted strength and effectiveness in finding solutions to the medical and caregiving matters that need to be addressed, and the grief and strain of the situation.

May Kevin’s sister Cynthia be cured of the hallucinations and delusions that have afflicted her, and freed from emotional distress. May she be safely healed of the physical condition that has provoked her emotions; and may she be healed of the spiritual condition that brings her to be so unsettled by it. May she come to feel calm and secure in her physical body, regardless of its level of health.

May Frank R. Hartman, who lost his house in the Altadena fire, and all who have been affected by the larger conflagration be blessed and healed.

May Corey Benton, who is currently in hospital and whose throat tumor has grown around an artery and won't be treated surgically, be healed of throat cancer. Healing work is also welcome. Judith be blessed and healed for a complete recovery from cancer.

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

comments.title: Money
comments.content:
Hi JMG
What would be a healthy and wise understanding of, and relationship with, money?
Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content:
There is, and cannot be, just one answer to this question! Your understanding of and relationship with money can never be the same as mine, or anyone else's, because each of us is a unique person with an equally unique set of needs, abilities, and potentials.

Money is particularly hard to cram into one-size-fits-all notions because it's just a set of abstract tokens that our culture uses to manage the distribution of wealth. Be it paper or shiny metal or magnetic charges in a memory chip, it isn't actually wealth -- it's a way of exchanging real wealth. Since it's abstract, it can be a very large number of useful things and an even larger number of dysfunctional things to different people. What is it to you? Only you can decide.

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content:
I heard of a millionaire, who, upon losing all of his money and finding himself deeply in debt, simply stated with total conviction “I am wealth” secure in his ability to make all of that money back.
I’m inclined to think that we all have some sort of inner wealth, which we can transform into outer wealth, which we can than transform into money.

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content: I know of some people who have used affirmations like that and gotten good results, and other people who've done the same thing and gotten very bad results. One size emphatically does not fit all!

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content:
Well thats synchronicity!

I just opened the comments, with a mind to asking about affirmations and money (sort of).

In the late 80's movie Wall Street, the famous phrase was, 'Greed is Good', so over the time of it's cinema release around the world, collectively, millions or at least many thousands of people, heard that phrase at the same time, and (possibly) absorbed it.

Also, the reviews in newspapers centred around that phrase, which many people read.

My question is, do you think this could have had an influence on what to me seems the turbo charged "greed" from that time to the present?

I don't mean that there haven't been other periods of greedy excess before, just that for this period, that phrase may have something to do with it?

Regards,
Helen in Oz

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content: Yes, I think that's quite possible.

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content:
This reminds me of the possibility that the plot arc of the Matrix trilogy ended up being a major "sigil" for transgender activism, because of where both authors were coming from ( https://screenrant.com/is-the-matrix-movie-trans-allegory-explained/ ), but I haven't figured out what I want to ask about that.

I guess there's one technical question. In one interview, at least in reference to the first movie, Lilly Wachowski mentions:

> It’s not like these were conscious decisions, but more like we’re finding our way instinctively as these two closeted trans women. So all those things that your receptors are buzzing about are completely valid. When people say, “Oh, it’s a trans allegory,” it’s like, “Yeah… it is, but we weren’t like, Hey, let’s write a trans allegory .” That’s not how it started. We were like, “Hey, let’s write this action film,” and then we got our trans all over it. [laughs]

Given the strength of the psychological forces involved, is this roughly as powerful as if it had been an intentional sigil-like working done by two relatively experienced magi? I'm not sure I even know how to frame the question meaningfully, since there would be so many variables involved.

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content: Not really. I'd say it's more that the transgender theme was already moving through the crawlspaces of collective consciousness, and seized on those movies as a vehicle. Remember that humans aren't the only entities who set such patterns in motion!

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content: I had also been thinking on what the analogously implied ethical concerns would be, about the magical consequences of producing culturally influential media while having "knowingly" not yet sufficiently prioritized examining and making conscious one's own hidden motives. I guess your point goes along with a picture in which such concerns would be reasonably limited in scope. (Like if such effects as there were would be "already priced in" as part of the "normal" give-and-take conditions that everyone might need to be resilient against to anyway.)

replies.title: Re: Money
replies.content: More or less, yes. This is one of the places where it's important to be aware of collective as well as individual karma.

replies.title: Re: Money is Stored Energy.
replies.content:
Stored energy can intentionally be used for construction or destruction. It can also be just squandered -- like setting a can of gasoline on fire in the street. In wisdom it can power the world the world (or, at least, your world). In ignorance it can cause huge, unexpected damage -- it can destroy you or anyone ignorant you give it to. (So don't give money to unwise people; it may well cause them more harm than letting them "suffer" without.)

In many circumstances, it can be exchanged for time. You can use it to buy labor or the labor of others. It can save your life, or the lives of those you love, in many cases.

The law of entropy also says that money which is idle, or not kept carefully, can simply waste away.

Money is stored energy. That is all.

-gnat

comments.title: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
comments.content:
Quite some time ago someone remarked on this forum that a statistically significant percentage of nurses caring for schizophrenics eventually come to believe that their patients are plagued by evil spirits.

Supposing this to be so, what about other deranged or insane people? How can one know whether or in what degree mental illness is caused by organic or psychological causes on the one hand, or spiritual factors on the other?

I am thinking, among other things, of Jesus casting out devils from a man afflicted by them. How many deranged people today are thus afflicted? How many could be, as it were, exorcised?

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content: It's a very complex issue. Dion Fortune, who trained as a psychotherapist, said that demonic activity is very often a cause of mental illness and even more often an additional factor in it. Telling the various causes of mental illness apart from one another is a difficult task, made even harder by our culture's kneejerk rejection of the existence of spirits.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content: Do you happen to recollect in what book she discusses this? I want to look into it.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content: I know Psychic Self-Defence is one place she talks about it.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content: Another interesting book that talks about this is "The Others Within Us" by Robert Falconer

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content: There's another book on this, "An Amazing Journey Into the Psychotic Mind - Breaking the Spell of the Ivory Tower" by Jerry Marzinsky, which you might find useful.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content:
Remember the part about "the last state of that man is worse than the first" (Mt 12). It is traditional to suppose that the metaphorical house from that passage being "swept and clean" is meant to connote that the metaphorical house is empty because God is still not dwelling there. I'm not sure how the evidence value of this passage and interpretational tradition should be taken to generalize outside Christian practice, but you should generally be very conservative about plans that involve an exorcised patient continuing to resist an evil spirit merely on their own power without some sort of religious or religiously-connected practice. (There was something about the Golden Dawn having standard instructions not to perform any magical practices above a certain level without invoking divine names, because such powerful forces directed by the human's unreflective nature without side correction would result in the human leaning into their own excesses and drifting off into crazytown. So, in a sense, even "natural forces"-based magical protective practices might have to be religiously-connected beyond a certain point: unexpected features of human nature alone would be already enough to be effectively equivalent to "evil spirits" for the purpose of overcoming such protection as those practices may provide, let alone independent spirits who actually have experience with manipulating people in such directions.)

Really, if you think about the structures of causal vulnerability within human minds and societies the way a security professional would, and then line up those hypothetical vulnerabilities against the observed capabilities of spirits, it's obvious that the evil spirits we have (along with most of the other kinds of possible supernatural influences, really) have to be in some kind of equilibrium of being restrained already, presumably so people can get experience with them in a lower-stakes setting within a human-intelligible narrow vicinity of "normality". So questions about "exorcisms" and other treatments are to some extent just questions about causing variations in the degree of the restraints that already exist. There's a question as to whether those restraining forces are themselves divine in origin, but I'm confused enough that I'm not sure such a question would turn out to have a meaningful answer even if we did understand how the restraining equilibrium stayed in place. Certainly divine forces seem to mostly act in line with such restraints, whether or not it was their idea.

"People of the Lie", by M. Scott Peck, is a slightly flawed but worthwhile empirical report from a psychotherapist about a few non-schizophrenic patients whose trapped patterns of behavior didn't respond in expected ways to therapy until they received an exorcism.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia, insanity and evil spirits
replies.content:
Here's another view from Hazrat Inayat Kha, for what it's worth:

https://wahiduddin.net/mv2/IV/IV_51.htm

It is our everyday experience that every little change we find in ourselves, in our thought and feeling, in our word and movement, is also caught by us unconsciously from someone else. The more intelligent person, the person who is more living, is more susceptible to reflections; and if that person happens to be more spiritual, then he has reflections from both sides, from the earth and from the other side. You will find in him a change every day and every moment, a certain change which is again the phenomenon of reflection.

A person in an abnormally negative state also gets reflections from the inner world. You will find in the insane asylum many cases of mediums. The physicians may not acknowledge it, and they may call it some kind of hallucination, but it is really a mediumistic soul which is open to another reflection from the other side. But, as Omar Khayyam says, 'A hair's breadth divides false from true.'

Such is the condition between normal and abnormal. It is just a hair's breadth. It is the same faculty, the same condition of spirit that could make one illuminated, and just a little difference can make a person insane.

and: https://wahiduddin.net/mv2/IV/IV_14.htm

There are no doubt many physical causes of various aspects of insanity, but a keen study of the subject will prove that insanity is mostly due to mental causes. Some lack of balance caused by the intensity or excess of a certain thought and feeling is found to be at the root of every cause of insanity. The physician fails to cure such cases, especially he who traces the cause of insanity in its outer manifestations and in the physical body. Every cause has an external effect, and yet it is a mistake to take the effect for the cause. It is not generally medicine or even surgical operations or any external application that can be of great use. It is more the work of a healer than of a physician to cure insanity.

Like every disease, insanity could easily be cured in its earlier stage. And it is again the work of the healer to recognize the signs of insanity in their primary state; for mostly such signs are not noticed in a person, or they are passed over as 'something funny' or 'queer'. The first step towards healing insanity is to get at the root of the complaint by association with the subject; and as soon as the root of the complaint is touched a great relief is brought, even before healing. Naturally, insanity being a mental disease; thought-power alone is the remedy for it.

Loss of memory, confusion, puzzlement, instantaneous temper and passion, all these are signs of the beginning of insanity. Insanity is inherited from the family, but it can also be traced in several weaknesses and vices, among which drink and fondness for drugs, unnatural habits, too much worry, anxiety, and allowing melancholy thoughts to develop in the nature. These are all things that cause insanity.

The work of the healer is first to detect the primary indication of insanity, and that is loss of memory. It is caused by weakness of the mind. The mind has not sufficient power to bring forward the thought entrusted to it at the command of the will. It is this, which may be called loss of memory, and it must be healed and cured in its very beginning. The primary stage is marked by an extreme activity of mind, which results in extreme thoughtless anger or passion. Then when its spell is passed repentance comes. This should be avoided at its beginning. A guilty conscience, fear of consequences, doubting tendencies. All such things are like fuel to the fire of insanity. A pure, thankful, useful life, a constant thought of appreciating things and avoiding blaming things and people and conditions, all these help to keep away the germ of insanity.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You’ve discussed before on your blog how polytheists had to act discreetly in their practices as Europe increasingly Christianized. I may have to do the opposite - work within a monotheistic organization while having spiritual experiences that don’t fit within the traditions of that organization. (I don’t label myself as necessarily polytheistic, because I don’t care for the label and don’t find my spiritual experiences fitting within any particular box, but my experiences are certainly more consistent with polytheism than monotheism.) This means presenting myself as outwardly monotheistic in beliefs and practices, but inwardly having different beliefs. This may even apply to romantic partnership, for all I know. Do you have a recommendation for maintaining this sort of double identity?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's been very common for the last fifteen hundred years; remember that for every polytheist pagan who was persecuted in Europe by the Christians, there was at least one Gnostic, Jew, or Christian heretic who got the same treatment. I know of no way to do it that doesn't cause psychological damage, and I'd strongly encourage you to walk away, even if that involves significant cost to yourself.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I would not enquire unless I feel genuinely nudged in a spiritual manner towards this route. The other options I can see seem inconsolable to me, strange as it might sound. My situation is unique. I don’t feel called upon to change my inner beliefs necessarily at this time, but I feel a sense of, “it’s this or it’s somewhere not good for your soul’s development.” I have had good contacts with Divine beings previously, and I’d be working for an institution connected with the Divine, so I trust the destination.

I am aware of the psychological challenges involved. I think that’s the point.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In that case, do what you feel is best, but I don't have any advice for you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My personal experience: the more you fake it, the harder it gets. Eventually that life becomes frustrating and truly exhausting. For me, it also became harder to follow my intuition, because part of the ordeal was like gaslighting myself on a regular basis.

I don't recommend it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I want first to thank you for your continued generous donation of time in your very busy days to answer our questions!

A quick question: my dad had a near death experience a few weeks back (he's 94) and the next day spent a couple of hours struggling to articulate to me what he had experienced. He saw his whole life in review, as usual, but he said that the judgement of our actions can only be understood in the context of the pattern (he really stressed the pattern of what we are) that was established while we were being formed in the womb, and that the right or wrong of the moment by moment play of our life cannot be established without that context. Moreover, he repeatedly said that we judge ourselves. We are our own judge of our life, being able finally to see clearly the context in which it occurred.

One of the examples he gave, (any devout French-Canadian Catholic will sympathize with this), is that boys are shaped to have curiosity. It's just part of the pattern that a young boy will naturally be curious about what his body can do and will explore it. He was talking about masturbation, which according to the church was a mortal sin, damning him to hell forever. What a relief for him to know that wasn't true! My dad has carried an unimaginable amount of guilt his whole life for absolutely everything that has happened to everyone he has ever met (ok, I'm exaggerating a bit but you get the idea.) My dad has always been a beautiful soul, full stop, but he lived with a lot of unfortunate beliefs.

Interestingly, he became hyperaware of the meaning of each moment with other people in the present, whoever they are.

My question is whether, like a PHD student internalizing the teacher, when we get to the level where we judge ourselves after death, we might be close to escaping the wheel of being human? And a bonus question if you have time, what does this mean for our notions of karma? I'm thinking we have no idea what our life's karma will actually be until we can see the whole in context, free of our learned cultural and social understanding of what is right and wrong, and also free of a set of laws that applies equally to everyone regardless, but instead according to the pattern that was set for us individually as we became incarnated as humans.

Thanks!
-Myriam

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is how karma works. It's not that some outside force or being sits in judgment over you; it's that from the perspective of the higher self, it's clear -- pleasantly or painfully as the case may be -- just what each action of your life really meant, and what you will have to do to balance things out. The fact that your father returned with a clear memory of this process does indeed suggest that he's basically done with material incarnation; he has a memory of seeing things from the perspective of his own higher self, and so the boundary between lower and higher self is very close to dissolving if it hasn't done so already. He should be fine when he does finally shed his material body.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That fits very well with what I know of my father! There is no doubt in my mind that he will watch over us lovingly and protectively when he is gone, as he is doing while yet alive.

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: An interesting sidelight here: Once again Dante (he certainly had esoteric connections, whether the Fideli de Amore or the Fratelli Obscuri, or the Templars, as Rene Guenon speculated); he relates in the Inferno that the souls who are heading to Hell come before King Minos for judgement and sentence to the requisite place of punishment, and the souls confess their misdeeds, indeed they are eager to do so, and so they essentially determine their own punishment in the Inferno.<q></q>

comments.title: CGD Bardic work
comments.content:
Good morning Mr. Greer and fellow MMers,

After most of a year of feeling like the slow kid in the class, I completed my Ovate examination and initiation in the CGD system last week. Now I am in the process of figuring out where to go from here, and I was hoping to get some advice from others who have worked through the system.

I know that I would like to continue on with CGD work, but my living situation precludes my doing any ceremonial stuff more complex than the Rite of the Central Ray at my usual time of practice early in the morning when I wake up. I'm fortunate enough to have enough flexibility in my work schedule to create an hour or two of time per week where I wont be constrained in practice, but in reading over the workings of the grade, I was unable to decide if 1-2 hours would be enough to make progress with these rituals.

I was hoping that someone else who has gone through the Bardic grade could advise me on their experiences with the workings, and whether I would just be spinning my wheels with a limited window like that, or whether I am psyching myself out over nothing. I realize that there isn't a one-size-fits-all nature to this sort of practice(one of the main realizations I've had over the last year is just how much cultural box-checking I've absorbed into my life, rather than living in growth and unfolding), but it would definitely help me to hear about other's experiences.

I also had a technical question about the Rite of the Central Ray. Should step 1 of the rite be alternated between the LBRP and the LIRP, or does the rite itself take the place of the LIRP for bringing in energies and maintaining balance thereby?

Thanks again for hosting the MM forum JMG.

replies.title: Re: CGD Bardic work
replies.content:
1) You may want to delay the full Bardic Grade work until your living situation changes. In the meantime, you can certainly take up the Rite of the Central Ray and study the Bardic Grade lectures, and see what else you can fit in as circumstances permit.

2) You always do the LBRP and then the Central Ray. The latter is an invocation and so nicely balances the banishing.

replies.title: Re: CGD Bardic work
replies.content:
Thank you for the response.

I suspected that would be the answer I would get, given how useful repeated practice of the Ovate temple opening and closing was for learning it all pat, but the elemental workings all sounded so interesting when I read them, I was hoping they might be amenable to cram sessions :P

Ah well, given my rate of progress during the Ovate grade, it might well be that my little one will be out of the danger zone by the time I work my way through all the material to meditate on anyway.

Would DF's The Mystical Qabalah be a comparable study book to the three listed in the Tree of Life section of the Bardic lectures? It has the beneficial quality of being a book I already possess.

Thanks again for sharing your knowledge with all of us.

replies.title: Re: CGD Bardic work
replies.content: Yes, you can certainly use Fortune's book as helpful reading. As for your little one, the process of bringing a spirit into material incarnation is the most important magical working you can do, so the time won't be wasted.

replies.title: Re: CGD Bardic work
replies.content:

comments.title: Group Karma
comments.content:
What is the best way to think of group karma?

It seems to me that it should all resolve to individual karma, and if anything souls can migrate between groups to resolve their own karma.

As an example: if there's a planet with two types of people, and with one group in power mistreating the other, I could imagine individuals from the group in power participating in that mistreatment reincarnating as members of the less powerful group to resolve their karma. The group itself wouldn't be resolving its karma, but the situation of the groups themselves provide an avenue for individuals to do so.

replies.title: Re: Group Karma
replies.content: Ah, but most people don't really have their own thoughts, feelings, and motives -- they think, feel, and desire as their society teaches them. Thus they don't end up with much individual karma; it's all part of the collective identity, and so they tend to get clobbered by collective karma at intervals. (Wars, plagues, and other disasters are among the ways this plays out.) As individual souls develop the ability to think their own thoughts, feel their own feelings, and choose their own motivations, they accrue individual karma, and this begins the process of separating them from the collective and launching them into the process of spiritual development, which is always and only individual in nature.

replies.title: Re: Group Karma
replies.content: Does this means that the more one's thoughts, feelings and motives are one's own, and different from the group's, the less one will be affected by the group's collective karma?

replies.title: Re: Group Karma
replies.content: Yes, that's exactly what it means. There are benefits to being on the fringes!

replies.title: Re: Group Karma
replies.content:
Interesting... I guess the foundational question I should have asked here is how close to one's "group" do souls tend to reincarnate, and how those dynamics work.

I'm thinking of this in a more cosmopolitan way for my own spiritual development. I'm a white man in America in this life but if my karma is best worked through by being a Chinese woman living in Italy in the next life I see no reason why I wouldn't take that one.

But, thinking about family karma, I've heard enough about family members that tend to reincarnate in the same family, take turns as parent/child, and work things out that way. That sort of dynamic might extend to the group (and I wouldn't be surprised if I'm in a multi-life dance with some members of my family!)

In terms of your response I have to admit I have a revulsion to the idea that most people don't really have their own thoughts, feelings, and motives... I only have direct access to my own consciousness, occasionally have the thought of being the only one who "gets it" in a sea of NPCs, but it seems like that's a mindset I should be evolving myself out of, and it's only respectful to assume everyone has equally rich but totally different internal lives. But, thinking about it as a process we're all going through is a bit kinder: we're all working our way towards an "individual development stage" and are probably all at different points across multiple dimensions of this process. I see lots of places where I don't fall for groupthink, and probably am missing a lot of places where I'm as blind as the worst examples I see around me.

Lots to meditate on. Thank you for this space and your response!

replies.title: Re: Group Karma
replies.content: Some souls reincarnate in the same family; other souls reincarnate very far afield. It really varies from person to person. As for groupthink, we all have it, but some are more submerged in it than others -- that doesn't make them bad or inferior, it's simply a measure of how far they've come along the journey we're all making.

comments.title: Korean Shamanic (Muism) Sphere of Protection

Opening
Bring hands to the forehead.
“Hwanin, Bright Father of Heaven, Eternal and Wise.”
Lower hands to the belly.
“Jisin, Great Earth Mother, Sustainer of Life.”
Raise arms outward, palms open.

Cross arms over chest.

Elemental Invocations
Air

Closing

replies.title: Re: Korean Shamanic (Muism) Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
I'm not sure this isn't standard, but you mentioned five elements and I only count four.

I don't know how close Muist tradition is to Yijing symbolism (the Korean flag notwithstanding), but you may be in a position to experiment with a possibility I noticed a few years ago: a cube oriented vertically along one diagonal with a Yijing trigram for each corner, with heaven on top, earth on the bottom, and each edge varying one line, so that the downward-pointing inscribed tetrahedron has all the Yin element-substances and the upward-pointing inscribed tetrahedron has all the Yang force-patterns.

The materials used involve quartz, which is piezoelectric (responds to pressure by giving off energy) and plenty of granite.

1. Only an scientifically advanced civilization (clearly more advanced than ours) could have built such a structure with no evidence of fossil fuels anywhere

5. The ancient Egyptians (and most likely all the other pyramid/mound builders) knew a great deal more about the etheric/energy plane than we will ever know.

replies.title: Re: Egyptian pyramids
replies.content:
Yes, some almost fully lost traditions look at the pyramids, particularly the great pyramid, as the last and greatest surviving example of etheric engineering. There's a reason they, and the Temple of Solomon, feature so deeply in the bits and pieces that have been passed down. The Egyptians were the masters of this, although the sources I've read says that no, they were just one of the the surviving locations of the megalithic technology, which is far more ancient.

The critical feature seems to be the recognition that the shape of something - be it a crystalline structure in a material, the object itself (the pyramid), or even a radio wave, has a particular quality or "colour" based on that shape - independent from any physical form. This quality appears to be universal. It's very much the case with music and ritual - there's a geometry of ritual, architecture and music that matters.

Our traditions have kept the astral component of this, but may have forgotten that the etheric form has a very real effect as well. There are very good reasons for this secrecy too. It does however seem to be that a deep dive into architecture, music, and radio engineering gets you to the same place.

From what I know (and I'm trying to get my manuscript finished and off to a publisher in the two month or so), the structures functioned without rituals (and still do), although I don't doubt that rituals enhanced things somewhat - perhaps for the individual more than the structure.

When I first went down this rabbit hole after trying to trace the traditions and references in our host's books, it did feel like a literal Indiana Jones expedition, but I had a certain sceptical incredulity about it - it couldn't be real, just a bunch of French and German nutters with too much time and money on their hands.

Then I built a very crude form of one of the devices, and tested it on a couple of sensitive subjects. They felt the "colour", felt the energy. It's real.

It's not missing from physics, but not generally useful in the context of the things we are looking for, and almost impossible to detect.

The one line of "normal" physics is biophoton research, ultra weak photos with say, about 9x10-19 joules of energy per photon.

A few weeks ago, I asked you about a discussion I had, with a thinker you identified as an unusual form of Gnostic. He said it represents several concepts, one of which is movement/action (which is impossible within the false totality), and that this is not a cardinal direction or axis, but a centrifugal spin. The forward/clockwise swastika represents movement in time, towards the Demiurge's totality, while the reversed/counterclockwise swastika (eg nazi) is an unwinding towards transcendence (I added the relevant symbols to the title).

replies.title: Re: 卐 the swastika 卍
replies.content:
Ah, symbolism! Within very broad limits, so rich and simple a geometrical symbol as the swastika can be made to mean just about anything. Here are some examples of this very common decorative design in American products from before 1933:
[IMAGE:0]
In this guy's symbolic language, the two swastikas can certainly have those meanings, but I don't recommend trying to use that set of interpretations anywhere else. Symbolism is no more univocal in meaning than language -- the word "gift" in German means "poison," for example, but I don't suggest you give people poisons for Christmas and birthdays!

replies.title: Re: 卐 the swastika 卍
replies.content:

According to the Gnostic, there are two types of symbols: most are provisional symbols, useful for communication, while others point the way to higher, sacred knowledge for those with a transcendent essence. If I understand correctly, you're denying the existence of timeless symbols, and insisting that all are merely provisional?

replies.title: Re: 卐 the swastika 卍
replies.content: I'm denying that it's possible for human beings to express the true meanings of symbols in any language we're currently capable of using. Doubtless the swastika has some eternal meaning, but as Lao Tsu put it, "the Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao;" once you put anything in words, you've condemned it to the provisional level.

replies.title: Re: 卐 the swastika 卍

So creating a mental body could be one step, and fixing the mental body (corresponding to enlightenment) could be another?  Hopefully this makes sense; can clarify if needed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Um, I think you've misunderstood what I said. Here's the conversation:

*******

Athaia:

"In that case, enlightenment doesn't seem to be a requirement to get to the post-human stage, does it? Considering the sheer number of humans (even the lower numbers of the past), we'd have scores of enlightened beings in their twelfth (or 33rd) incarnation otherwise.

"Is the deciding factor "simply" to have developed a fully functional mental body, and that's all that's needed? That would explain why there are so many less than benevolent disembodied beings hanging around..."

Me:

"Bingo. Remember that material incarnation is the prenatal state of the soul. Once we're born into the discarnate state, we begin our real lives, and we have much, much more to learn thereafter."

*******

The point is not that there's some sort of ladder in which making the mental body is one rung, fixing it is another, enlightenment comes after that, and so on. The experience that's called "enlightenment" is not mandatory and it's not universal. It's a specific neurological spasm that jolts the mental apparatus good and hard, and in the right setting, can lead to greatly expanded perception. In and of itself, though, it's not that different from orgasm, and as Robert Graves pointed out once, the obsession with that experience can very easily devolve into a refined form of masturbation. (Is an obsessive craving for enlightenment really any better than any other obsessive craving?)

The process of forming and establishing the mental body, by contrast, is a gradual one. Certain spiritual disciplines, especially meditation, can provide great assistance in that process. The experience of enlightenment can also help, but only when it happens in a suitable context and doesn't end up inflating the ego to fantastic size. (As the Zen saying has it, a person who obtains immediate complete enlightenment is an arrow shot straight into Hell.) But enlightenment itself is a tool for the work, not its fulfillment.

Oh, and the Dolmen Arch reference to "fixing the self" is simply a name for an exercise, not a stage in spiritual growth. I recommend reading the book again...

comments.title: visualizing vs daydreaming
comments.content:
One often hears of the power of visualization -- you know, the law of attraction, picture it and make it happen. Even sports coaches talk about visualizing a perfect throw or perfect match. I know it works very well for many people, though I know equally well that there are limits.

Here's my problem: every time I've tried to visualize success in something, or the steps to success in something, I find I've just wasted all my energy on the visuals and nothing actually happens. Not only do I not get what I want, I find I lose all motivation to actually work towards what I want.

Rather than wasting time imagining success, or even the steps to success, I have to deliberately not think about things and *do* them. So it occurs to me that maybe this visualization stuff doesn't work for everyone, OR perhaps I'm doing it wrong. Maybe I'm just daydreaming about my goals.

There are a three questions here, to be explicit:
1) is it fair to say that daydreaming fritters away mental energy uselessly, while visualization can focus it to results?
If yes,
1a) What's the difference? and,
1b) How can I stop daydreaming and start properly visualizing?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: visualizing vs daydreaming
replies.content: All this varies from person to person. "Creative visualization" works well for some people and very, very badly for others. In your case, I suggest that it just doesn't work for you, and you might want to try other options.

replies.title: Re: visualizing vs daydreaming
replies.content:
OP here.
Thank you! You answered a more important question than the ones I thought to ask.

Would it be greedy to ask a followup? I'm curious what other options you are alluding to here.

replies.title: Re: visualizing vs daydreaming
replies.content:
Some people find it more effective to use verbal affirmations -- that's my standard tool for things like this, since I naturally think in words. (You can find a good basic set of instructions here.) Some people find it more effective to use a sigil, which represents the desired state without directly showing it -- you can make one by writing a sentence that states your intention, crossing out all the vowels and all repeating letters, and turning the resulting letters into a glyph like this:
[IMAGE:0]
(That's from this article, which is a good introduction to the process.)

There are many other ways to do the thing. You can visualize a tarot card that represents your intention, or what have you. You'll want to experiment and see what works best for you.

comments.title: A cat
comments.content: I happen to have a cat. The thing is, he's nothing like any cat I or my wife ever had. He's way smarter, knows his own name, manifests jealousy, manages to communicate what he wants pretty well, and we've witnesses he showing one of his new toys to a cat-sitter (like a kid showing his new toy to a friend). Makes me wonder if he isn't at the cusp of humanness...

replies.title: Re: A cat
replies.content: And possibly not for the first time. It's a standard Druid teaching that souls that mess up badly enough can drop down to the animal level again for a refresher course.

replies.title: Re: A cat
replies.content: Yeah I know right? I don't like thinking he might be a flunker though 😕 I'd rather think he's ahead of the curve. But it is a possibility for sure. If that's the case, may we help him make the most of it 😌

replies.title: Re: A cat
replies.content: Going back and doing remedial work as someone's dog (or cat) is kind of embarrassing, when you think about it. Still, it's much preferable to the soul dissolving back into the source, as you described on a previous Monday.

replies.title: Re: A cat
replies.content: Nah, it's just one incident in a soul's journey. If you try to jump over a stream, and land face first in the mud, it's a good idea to go back a good ways and get a running start so you can make the jump. That's what souls do when they go back to animal bodies for a few lives.

comments.title: Meditation on Daath
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thank you for providing this discussion space and taking the time to answer our questions. I hope you're well!

I'm currently working through Gareth Knight's Practical Guide to Qabalistic Symbolism. For each chapter, I do a series of daily discursive meditations on the text and the various symbols he associates with each Sephirah. Once I've worked my way through each chapter, I do a scrying exercise (based on Experience of the Inner Worlds) prompted by that Sephirah'a God Name. The resulting active imagination provides the raw material for my next few days of meditation.

My question: I've just started the Daath chapter, and Knight gives some warnings suggesting that this Sephirah or quasi-Sephirah may be particularly risky to meditate on. Do you think I should modify my approach for this chapter in any way? I.e., are there specific themes/symbols/images of Daath that I should avoid meditating on? Should I skip the scrying or the meditation or both? Or should I just proceed as normal while keeping the risks in mind?

Thanks,

Fred N.

replies.title: Re: Meditation on Daath
replies.content: I didn't find Daath especially risky; in any case, the method you've set out seems sensible and safe to me.

comments.title: To temper the fire
comments.content:
My horoscope is chockablock with the element of fire. When karma hits, which it has done a lot lately, it’s like a blow torch.

My magic background, of fairly modest attainments, is in Hellenic Golden Dawn. I assume that invoking Jupiter and other moist planets - Venus, the Moon - may help. Are these the right ones? And is there anything else I can do to temper the karmic firestorm?

replies.title: Re: To temper the fire
replies.content:
First, make sure the watery planets aren't afflicted -- if they are, you'll just make things worse. If they're well dignified, Jupiter as well as Venus and the Moon can be invoked for this purpose. If Saturn is well dignified, he's also good -- he's not watery but he's cold and very calm -- but don't invoke him unless he's well dignified, or you'll regret it.

Second, remember that karma isn't going to go away just because you don't enjoy it, and the fiery aspect of its nature may also be karmic. If you embrace it willingly, it'll be easier.

comments.title: Significance of colors
comments.content:
I'm curious about the significance of colors and how much impact changing a color makes.

Specifically for the red bag amulet and dice for geomancy divination. Is using a purple or dark red bag going to cause problems? What about a black bag, or green, etc.? What does the ted do in a red bag?

Similar question for geomatic dice. A friend gave me some very nice dice. I have some wooden dice in red, yellow, green, and blue. Would using jade, amethyst, and othe stones cause problems since they are not the right colors.

replies.title: Re: Significance of colors
replies.content: Those have different factors behind them. The color of the bag in the amulet is part of the spell, and changing it will weaken the connection to the egregor. The color of the dice is simply for convenience -- it keeps you from getting confused about which die goes with which line -- and you can use anything else that you want.
